content.text: are AI systems that execute tasks spanning hours, days, or even weeks — maintaining a coherent plan and consistent state across thousands of individual steps. Unlike single-turn agents that answer one question and stop, or short-horizon agents that handle tasks completable in a few dozen tool calls, long-horizon agents must decompose ambitious goals into trackable subgoals, manage what they remember, checkpoint their progress, and recover from mid-task failures — all without human intervention on every step.

A long-horizon agent accepts a high-level goal — "implement this feature across our codebase", "survey the literature on topic X and write a report", or "process and reconcile last quarter's invoices" — and pursues it autonomously over an extended period. The defining property is

: the task cannot be completed in a single context window, a single session, or a single burst of sequential tool calls. The agent must persist intermediate state, track what it has and has not done, and regularly re-evaluate whether it is still on track toward the original goal.
Contrast with the spectrum of agent types:
one LLM call, one response. No tool use, no memory, no planning. Handles questions that fit entirely in one prompt.
a ReAct or tool-calling loop lasting seconds to minutes — typically 5–50 tool calls. The entire interaction fits in one context window.
a structured loop lasting minutes to days — hundreds to tens of thousands of tool calls. State is persisted externally; context is managed by compression and retrieval.
a long-horizon agent augmented with explicit human approval gates at defined risk thresholds — combining autonomy with oversight.

The highest-value tasks in software engineering, scientific research, and business process automation are inherently long-horizon. A software engineer implementing a non-trivial feature touches dozens of files, runs tests repeatedly, reads documentation, and iterates — often over several hours. A researcher synthesising a literature review reads hundreds of papers, extracts key claims, cross-references findings, and refines an argument over days. These tasks have resisted automation because they require sustained, coherent effort that exceeds what a single LLM call — or even a short agentic loop — can deliver.

Long-horizon agents matter because they are the path from "AI that helps with steps" to "AI that owns complete deliverables". They unlock autonomous execution of complex professional tasks, dramatically reducing the time humans spend on work that is high-effort but tractable — the kind of knowledge work that is valuable precisely because it is hard to do at scale.
handles tasks humans cannot do in one sitting, freeing human time for high-judgment decisions.
a planner can spawn independent subagents to work on different subtasks concurrently, compressing wall-clock time dramatically.
a well-implemented long-horizon agent does not skip steps due to fatigue or distraction — it executes its plan with consistent attention.
checkpointing means failures at step 847 do not lose the work of steps 1–846 — the agent resumes from the last checkpoint.
one orchestrator can manage a fleet of specialised subagents, scaling effort proportionally to task size.
small misunderstandings or incorrect assumptions made early in a long plan compound into large deviations by the end. A wrong interpretation at step 3 can corrupt everything downstream.
the agent cannot fit the full history of a 10,000-step task into one context window. Deciding what to keep, compress, and discard is itself a hard problem.
when a tool call fails at step 500, the agent must decide whether to retry, replan the affected branch, or abort. Recovery logic is complex to implement correctly.
humans must review thousands of agent actions. Deciding which actions to audit, when to trust intermediate results, and when to intervene is a sociotechnical problem.
without explicit mechanisms to track the original goal, agents can incrementally optimise for proxies that diverge from the intended outcome.
A production long-horizon agent combines three mechanisms:

The goal is decomposed into a tree of subgoals at multiple levels of abstraction. A top-level goal ("build a web scraper for competitor pricing data") becomes a plan with milestones ("design schema", "implement scraper", "add error handling", "write tests", "deploy"). Each milestone decomposes further into concrete actionable steps. The executor works at the leaf level but the planner tracks progress at the milestone level — this separation prevents the executor from losing sight of the big picture.
the current context window — the last N tokens of observations, tool outputs, and reasoning. Volatile; lost between sessions.
a chronological log of what the agent did and observed. Stored externally (database or vector store) and retrieved by step index or recency.

distilled facts and conclusions from past steps. Stored as a knowledge base, retrieved by semantic similarity. Example: "the project uses SQLAlchemy 2.0, not the 1.x API" — a fact that should inform every database-related step even 500 steps later.

Because context windows are finite, the agent must compress older history. A simple but effective approach is to summarise every completed milestone into a paragraph and discard the raw step logs. The compression ratio for a block of history is:
is realistic for structured task history: a 20,000-token milestone log can often be compressed to a 2,000-token summary without losing state critical for subsequent steps. The key is to preserve

attrs.alt: Long-Horizon Agent Architecture: Goal -> Planner -> Task Queue -> Executor Loop (with Memory, Tools, Checkpoints, Human Gate) -> Evaluator -> Done or loop back

content.text: The architecture has a clear data flow: the
translates the high-level Goal into an ordered Task Queue. The
picks tasks from the queue, calls tools, records results in Memory, and saves Checkpoints. The
inspects whether the goal has been met — if not, it feeds back to the Task Queue (possibly triggering replanning). Human Gates catch high-risk steps before they execute.
the task has a clear terminal condition (done = all tests pass, report is submitted, invoices are reconciled), the total work exceeds one context window, and subtasks can be individually verified.
if analysing 50 papers can be split across 10 subagents running in parallel, a long-horizon agent with fan-out is far more efficient than a sequential single-agent loop.
the task is completable in 5–30 tool calls, all state fits in one context window, and the task does not require maintaining a plan across sessions.
the agent will take irreversible actions (deploy to production, send emails to clients, delete data) that require explicit human sign-off at defined thresholds.
How to Implement a Long-Horizon Agent
The core loop is straightforward to describe but tricky to implement robustly. Here are the five steps:

call the LLM once with the high-level goal and ask it to produce a structured plan — a list of milestones, each with a list of tasks and a success condition. Store this plan in a persistent data structure (JSON file, database row).
maintain a task queue with status fields (pending, in-progress, done, failed). Update status atomically after each step.
after completing each milestone (or every N steps), serialise the full agent state to durable storage. Include: current plan, task statuses, compressed memory, and any artifacts produced so far.

when a task fails, load the last checkpoint and decide: retry the same step, skip it, or replan the affected branch. Implement exponential backoff for transient failures (API rate limits, network errors).
after each milestone, run an evaluator that checks whether the goal's success conditions are met. If not, feed back to the planner for course correction.

import json
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class Task:
    task_id: str
    description: str
    status: str = "pending"  # pending | in_progress | done | failed
    result: str = ""
    attempts: int = 0

@dataclass
class AgentPlan:
    goal: str
    tasks: list[Task] = field(default_factory=list)
    checkpoint_path: str = "checkpoint.json"

    def save_checkpoint(self):
        data = {
            "goal": self.goal,
            "tasks": [
                {"task_id": t.task_id, "description": t.description,
                 "status": t.status, "result": t.result, "attempts": t.attempts}
                for t in self.tasks
            ]
        }
        with open(self.checkpoint_path, "w") as f:
            json.dump(data, f, indent=2)

    @classmethod
    def load_checkpoint(cls, path: str) -> "AgentPlan":
        with open(path) as f:
            data = json.load(f)
        plan = cls(goal=data["goal"], checkpoint_path=path)
        plan.tasks = [Task(**t) for t in data["tasks"]]
        return plan


def run_long_horizon_agent(
    plan: AgentPlan,
    executor: Callable[[Task], str],
    max_retries: int = 3,
):
    """Execute an AgentPlan, checkpointing after each task."""
    for task in plan.tasks:
        if task.status == "done":
            print(f"[skip] {task.task_id} already done")
            continue

        task.status = "in_progress"
        plan.save_checkpoint()

        for attempt in range(1, max_retries + 1):
            task.attempts = attempt
            try:
                result = executor(task)
                task.result = result
                task.status = "done"
                print(f"[done] {task.task_id}: {result[:80]}")
                plan.save_checkpoint()  # checkpoint on success
                break
            except Exception as exc:
                print(f"[fail] {task.task_id} attempt {attempt}: {exc}")
                if attempt == max_retries:
                    task.status = "failed"
                    plan.save_checkpoint()
                    raise RuntimeError(f"Task {task.task_id} failed after {max_retries} attempts") from exc
                time.sleep(2 ** attempt)  # exponential backoff


# --- Demo ---
def mock_executor(task: Task) -> str:
    # In production this calls an LLM or tool
    return f"Completed: {task.description}"

plan = AgentPlan(
    goal="Write a Python web scraper for product prices",
    tasks=[
        Task("t1", "Design the URL structure and data schema"),
        Task("t2", "Implement the HTTP fetch and HTML parse logic"),
        Task("t3", "Add error handling and exponential backoff"),
    ]
)

run_long_horizon_agent(plan, mock_executor)
Code Example: Research Agent with Progress Tracking

import anthropic
import json
from pathlib import Path

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def decompose_goal_into_plan(goal: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Ask the LLM to produce a structured research plan."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1000,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Create a step-by-step research plan for this goal: {goal}\n\n"
                "Return a JSON list of tasks. Each task: "
                '{"task_id": "t1", "description": "...", "output_type": "text"}'
            )
        }]
    )
    raw = response.content[0].text
    # Extract JSON from the response
    start = raw.index("[")
    end = raw.rindex("]") + 1
    return json.loads(raw[start:end])


def execute_research_step(task: dict, context: str) -> str:
    """Run a single research step, passing compressed prior context."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2000,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Prior research context (compressed):\n{context}\n\n"
                f"Current task: {task['description']}\n\n"
                "Complete this task thoroughly and return your findings."
            )
        }]
    )
    return response.content[0].text


def compress_context(completed_tasks: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Summarise completed tasks into a compact context block."""
    if not completed_tasks:
        return "(no prior steps)"
    summaries = []
    for t in completed_tasks:
        summaries.append(f"- {t['task_id']}: {t['description']} -> {t['result'][:200]}")
    return "\n".join(summaries)


def research_agent(goal: str, checkpoint_file: str = "research_checkpoint.json"):
    """Long-horizon research agent that resumes from checkpoints."""
    checkpoint = Path(checkpoint_file)

    if checkpoint.exists():
        state = json.loads(checkpoint.read_text())
        tasks = state["tasks"]
        print(f"Resuming from checkpoint - {sum(1 for t in tasks if t['status']=='done')} tasks done")
    else:
        print("Planning research...")
        raw_tasks = decompose_goal_into_plan(goal)
        tasks = [{**t, "status": "pending", "result": ""} for t in raw_tasks]
        checkpoint.write_text(json.dumps({"goal": goal, "tasks": tasks}, indent=2))

    for task in tasks:
        if task["status"] == "done":
            continue

        completed = [t for t in tasks if t["status"] == "done"]
        context = compress_context(completed)

        print(f"\nExecuting: {task['task_id']} - {task['description']}")
        task["status"] = "in_progress"
        checkpoint.write_text(json.dumps({"goal": goal, "tasks": tasks}, indent=2))

        result = execute_research_step(task, context)
        task["result"] = result
        task["status"] = "done"
        checkpoint.write_text(json.dumps({"goal": goal, "tasks": tasks}, indent=2))
        print(f"Done: {result[:150]}...")

    print("\n=== Research Complete ===")
    return tasks


# research_agent("Survey recent advances in protein structure prediction")

SWE-bench is a benchmark of 2,294 real GitHub issues from popular Python repositories. Solving a single SWE-bench issue is a canonical long-horizon agent task: the agent must understand the bug report, navigate a large unfamiliar codebase, reproduce the failure, implement a fix, ensure existing tests still pass, and write new tests for the fix — all without human assistance.
run the provided reproduction script, confirm the failure, read the traceback.
use grep, AST search, and test-run feedback to identify the file and function responsible.
read the surrounding code, type annotations, docstrings, and relevant tests to understand the intended behavior.
edit the identified file, run the reproduction case to confirm it passes.
run the full test suite, fix any regressions introduced by the change.
add tests that would have caught this bug earlier.

This workflow can take 50–200 tool calls across multiple context windows — a clear long-horizon task that requires persistent state, checkpointing between phases, and an evaluator that checks test-pass rate rather than just code generation quality.
Losing track of the original goal:

after 200 steps, the agent context is dominated by recent observations. Without explicitly re-injecting the goal statement into every few context windows, the agent gradually drifts toward locally optimal subgoal completion rather than the original intent.
compressing history too aggressively — or not at all — causes the agent to repeat work already done, or to contradict decisions made in earlier steps.
a flat list of 500 atomic steps with no milestone structure makes it impossible to track progress, replan partial failures, or communicate status to humans.
a crash at step 847 of an 850-step task that has no checkpoints means starting from scratch. Always checkpoint after each milestone, and ideally after each individual task.
retrying a failed step indefinitely without an escape condition wastes resources and blocks downstream tasks. Always set a max-retry ceiling and a fallback (skip, replan, or escalate to human).
executing all planned tasks without a loop to check whether the goal is actually met means the agent can complete its task list and still have failed the underlying objective.
always maintain at least two levels of abstraction — milestones and tasks. Track milestone completion separately from task completion.
persist state after every task, not just every milestone. Storage is cheap; re-doing 50 LLM calls is expensive.
include an explicit evaluation step after each milestone. The evaluator should check the goal's success conditions, not just whether the task list is empty.
include the original goal statement in the system prompt and in every planning prompt, even during execution. Never let it scroll out of context.
identify categories of actions that cannot be undone (sending emails, deploying to production, deleting data) and require explicit human approval before execution.
use deterministic, stable task IDs (not UUIDs). They make checkpoints human-readable and simplify debugging when a task fails.
Long-horizon agents face two distinct scalability challenges:

use a sliding window (keep recent N tokens), summarise completed milestones, and use RAG to retrieve specific past steps by semantic similarity. Aim for a compressed history representation that keeps the most relevant 20% of past context.
when the task graph has independent branches, dispatch them to parallel subagents. A 10-hour sequential task with 5 independent branches becomes a 2-hour task with parallel execution.
for tasks with high likely success probability, start them before their dependency is fully confirmed — rolling back is cheaper than the latency saved.
many research and analysis steps are idempotent. Cache tool call results by (function, arguments) hash to avoid redundant API calls on retry or resume.
a graph-based framework for building stateful agent loops. Nodes represent agent steps; edges represent transitions. Built-in checkpointing to SQLite or Postgres via the Checkpointer API.
Microsoft's multi-agent framework. Models agents as actors that communicate by message passing. The GroupChat pattern orchestrates multiple specialised agents for complex tasks.

durable execution engine that persists agent workflow state after every step. Provides automatic retry, backoff, and exactly-once execution guarantees — ideal for long-horizon agents that must survive crashes.

purpose-built for software engineering tasks. Combine a code search index (for fast file localisation), a sandboxed code executor (for test running), and a long-horizon planner that tracks issue -> plan -> implementation -> tests.

a task-list-based architecture where the LLM both executes tasks and generates new tasks based on results. Simple but effective for open-ended exploration tasks where the full plan cannot be known upfront.

LLMs occasionally fabricate facts. In a single-turn agent, a hallucination is contained. In a long-horizon agent, a hallucinated fact written to memory at step 10 can propagate and corrupt every downstream decision. Verify factual claims against ground truth sources before persisting them.

an agent can get trapped retrying the same failing step forever. Implement a global step budget (e.g. 10,000 tool calls max) and a stuck-detection heuristic (if the last N steps are all retries on the same task, escalate to human).
a misconfigured parallel agent can spawn thousands of subagents or make millions of API calls. Always enforce hard limits on concurrency, total API spend, and wall-clock duration.

code-executing agents must run in sandboxed environments (Docker containers, E2B sandboxes) with restricted network access and no persistent write access to production systems. Never run agent-generated code directly on the host.

web pages, files, and API responses read by the agent may contain malicious instructions designed to hijack the agent's behavior. Sanitise tool outputs and instruct the agent to treat external content as data, not instructions.
Long-horizon agents tackle tasks that span hours to days — too long for a single context window and requiring persistent state across many tool calls and LLM turns.
Hierarchical planning (goal -> milestones -> tasks) is the structural foundation that prevents goal drift and enables meaningful progress tracking.
Layered memory — working, episodic, and semantic — ensures the agent retains the right information at the right level of detail across its lifetime.
Aggressive checkpointing is non-negotiable: persist state after every task so crashes lose at most one step, not hours of progress.
An evaluator loop that checks goal success conditions — not just task-list completion — is what separates agents that deliver the right outcome from agents that look busy.
Error accumulation, stuck loops, and resource runaway are the primary failure modes — each requires an explicit mitigation: verification checkpoints, step budgets, and hard concurrency limits.
Parallel subagent execution is the primary lever for reducing wall-clock time on tasks with independent branches — a 10-hour sequential task can become a 2-hour parallel one.
LangGraph, AutoGen, and Temporal provide infrastructure for durable state, retry logic, and subagent coordination — use them rather than building task queues and checkpointing from scratch.
